email_subject: Re: Caltrain Corridor — Revised Soil Stabilization Figures
email_body:
Hi Marcus,

Attached is the revised cost estimate for the soil stabilization section of the Caltrain corridor drainage project. I've broken out the line items more granularly than the original submission so the variance is easier to audit.

Short version: the original figure assumed Class 2 aggregate base throughout. Actual site conditions on the southern segment call for lime treatment, which runs about 35% higher per ton. I flagged this in the March site report — happy to pull that up if it's useful context for the client conversation.

I'd recommend we get in front of this before the January 15 review rather than waiting for the meeting itself. I can be available Thursday afternoon or any time Friday if you want to walk through it.

Brandon
chat_snippet:
Me: you see the soil report come through for the southern segment?
Marcus: just now. that lime treatment number is not going to be fun to explain
Me: I know. I've been saying since March the assumptions were soft
Marcus: I know you have, Brandon
Marcus: I'm not going to relitigate it
Me: fair. Thursday work to prep for the client call?
Marcus: yeah Thursday 2pm. bring the March report too
Me: already pulled it

blog_title: Why I Keep a Wine Notebook (Even Though It's Embarrassing)
blog_excerpt:
The notebook started as a joke. I was at a small tasting room off Westside Road — maybe twelve people on a Saturday, a winemaker who clearly had no interest in small talk — and I found myself trying to describe what was in the glass. Not to anyone. Just to myself. I wrote 'forest floor, cherry, something like cold rain on old wood' and then immediately felt like I was performing a parody of a wine person.

I kept writing anyway. Twelve months later the notebook is half-full and I've started to notice something: the act of looking for language forces me to actually pay attention. To slow down. I spend most of my life moving fast and making quick assessments — that's useful in engineering, it's useful on a trail, it's useful in meetings where someone is trying to run out the clock on your objections. But a glass of wine doesn't reward speed. It rewards sitting still and letting a thing unfold. I'm not particularly good at that. The notebook is practice.
diary_entry:
December 28. Last real week of the year and I took Tuesday off and drove to Tennessee Valley. Parked at the lot, walked to the beach, sat on a rock for twenty minutes watching the surf. Nobody wanted anything from me. My phone was in my pocket and I didn't look at it once.

I've been thinking about what I actually want 2025 to look like. Not resolutions — I've made my peace with resolutions being mostly theater. Just a direction. I want to get to Johannesburg. I've been saying it for three years and Nadia has stopped pushing, which means she's either accepted it or given up, and I don't want it to be the second one. I want to run something harder than I've run before. I want to eat better during the week, not just on weekends when I'm motivated. Small stuff.

The ocean at the end of Tennessee Valley is always a little shocking — you walk through grassland and then suddenly it's just there, enormous and indifferent. I find that clarifying rather than depressing. Some things are bigger than your problems. The year wasn't bad. I should say that more clearly to myself.
resume_summary: Civil Engineer with 30+ years of experience in infrastructure design, drainage systems, and large-scale transportation projects in California. Proven track record managing complex multi-stakeholder projects from site assessment through construction oversight. Detail-oriented with strong command of soil mechanics, stormwater management, and regulatory compliance. Experienced in hybrid team environments and cross-functional project coordination.
cover_letter_line: My three decades in California civil engineering have given me a direct, evidence-based approach to infrastructure challenges — I'm the person who pulls the March site report in December because I documented the risk when I saw it.
recommendation_line: Brandon brings an uncommon combination of deep technical rigor and real-world pragmatism to every project he touches; when he flags a concern, experienced project managers learn quickly to take it seriously.
_note: SYNTHETIC SPECIMEN — generated persona — contains no real PII
